title: Ventricular Tachycardia — stable
oneLiner: Wide-complex tachycardia from the ventricles in a patient who is not yet peri-arrest — urgent recognition prevents deterioration to VF.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:** You’re called to a ward. A 64-year-old man known for ischaemic cardiomyopathy (ejection fraction 35%) reports sudden palpitations and mild light-headedness over the past 30 minutes. He is sitting comfortably, BP 105/60, HR 170, saturations 97%. His respiratory rate is 20, and he is able to complete sentences. ECG shows a regular wide-QRS tachycardia at 170/min. P waves are not clearly visible, but you notice a single narrow beat in the middle of the strip — a capture beat.

- **Dissect the vignette:**
  - *Palpitations + light-headedness* → tachyarrhythmia with reduced diastolic filling.
  - *Stable BP, no chest pain, no syncope* → the patient is currently compensating; this is **stable VT**.
  - *Known ischaemic cardiomyopathy* → re-entry around scar tissue is the favourite mechanism.
  - *Regular wide-QRS tachycardia* → narrow QRS (<0.12 s) rules out VT (unless fascicular or bundle branch block is present); wide QRS forces you to think VT first.
  - *Single narrow beat* → **capture beat** (a sinus beat that “captures” ventricle before next VT beat). Pathognomonic for VT. If you see it, stop — it’s VT.
  - *Absent P waves* → AV dissociation (atria and ventricles independent) is the gold-standard ECG sign of VT, but may be subtle.

- **Atypical presentations that trick you:**
  - **Painless VT:** An elderly diabetic with silent ischaemia — presents only with palpitations and mild dyspnoea. No chest pain, but blood pressure is normal. ECG misinterpreted as SVT with aberrancy.
  - **Fascicular VT (left septal VT):** The QRS is narrower (120–130 ms) and right bundle branch block morphology with left axis deviation. The patient is young, no structural heart disease. This can be mistaken for supraventricular tachycardia. It responds to verapamil (rare exception where it’s safe) but otherwise treat as VT.
  - **Bidirection VT (catecholaminergic polymorphic VT):** Seem irregular, alternating QRS axis. Usually triggers in children or young adults during exercise or emotional stress. Might be called “torsades” but is not – this is a different mechanism.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Definition:** Ventricular tachycardia is a rhythm originating from the ventricles, consisting of three or more consecutive beats at a rate >100/min (usually 140–200/min). The QRS is wide (≥0.12 s) because activation bypasses the His-Purkinje system. “Stable” means the patient has an adequate blood pressure, is alert, and has no signs of hypoperfusion (no chest pain, no syncope, no pulmonary oedema). This does **not** mean it’s low-risk — the throne is fragile.
- **Why it matters clinically:** VT is **common** — up to 10% of acute MI patients develop it. It can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation within seconds. The single greatest pitfall is misdiagnosing it as supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) with aberrancy and giving verapamil or adenosine, which can cause **hypotension, VF, or asystole**. In a stable VT patient, you have time (minutes to hours) to obtain a full ECG, check electrolytes, and assess the cause, but you must not waste that time.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **The story:** Normal ventricular activation spreads rapidly via the Purkinje system, producing a narrow QRS. In VT, the electrical impulse originates from an ectopic focus in the ventricular myocardium. Three main mechanisms drive this: **re-entry** (most common, especially in scarred heart), **abnormal automaticity** (ischaemia or catecholamines), and **triggered activity** (digoxin toxicity, long QT). In re-entry, a “circus” wave travels around a fixed or functional obstacle (e.g., infarct scar). The wave exits to depolarise the ventricles prematurely, then re-enters the circuit to sustain itself. The circuit or focus varies with each beat.

- **Why each clinical sign happens:**
  - *Wide QRS* — Ventricles are activated directly through myocardial muscle, not through the specialised conduction system. The depolarisation wave travels slowly and produces a broad, bizarre QRS.
  - *AV dissociation* — The atria and ventricles are controlled by separate pacemakers: the sinus node (slow, regular) and the ventricular focus (fast). The AV node is refractory to rapid retrograde conduction, so P waves appear randomly, unrelated to QRS complexes. You see this as p waves marching through a “sea” of QRS complexes.
  - *Capture beats* — Occasionally a sinus beat conducts through the AV node, “captures” the ventricles, and produces a normal narrow QRS in the middle of a wide-complex tachycardia. This proves the rhythm is VT.
  - *Fusion beats* — A sinus beat partially engages the ventricles at the same time as a VT beat. The QRS is intermediate in width and shape — a hybrid.
  - *Haemodynamic stability* — This depends on rate, LV function, and ventricular synchrony. If the VT is relatively slow (120–150), the heart has some filling time, and the patient remains perfused. If the LV is already impaired, even a moderate rate may cause hypotension. Conduction delay also leads to discoordinate contraction (like having a “mechanical wide QRS”), reducing stroke volume.

- **Connecting to pattern cues:** The capture beat you saw in the vignette is direct evidence of AV dissociation. The “regularity with subtle P waves” is a classic exam trap — if you don’t hunt for dissociation, you will call it SVT. The absence of response to adenosine (if given) also points to VT.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Specific Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Ischaemic** | Acute MI, prior infarct scar (most common) | “Previous MI” or “acute STEMI” |
| **Cardiomyopathy** | Dilated, hypertensive, hypertrophic | “LVEF 35% on echo” |
| **Electrolyte** | Hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hyperkalaemia | “Diuretic use, vomiting, CKD” |
| **Drug toxicity** | Digoxin, class 1a/1c antiarrhythmics, tricyclics | “History of atrial fibrillation on digoxin” |
| **Inherited** | Long QT syndrome, Brugada, ARVC | “Family history of sudden death” + specific ECG patterns |
| **Idiopathic** | RVOT VT, fascicular VT | Young, otherwise healthy, RBBB/LBBB morphology |
| **Metabolic/endocrine** | Hyperthyroidism, phaeochromocytoma | “Sweating, weight loss, hypertension” |

- **Most common cause:** Ischaemic heart disease (acute or chronic). Ask every patient with VT: “Have you ever had a heart attack or bypass surgery?”
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single scoring system replaces a careful ECG analysis, but the **Brugada Algorithm** (1991) helps differentiate VT from SVT with aberrancy. Key steps:
  1. Absence of RS complexes in all precordial leads? → VT.
  2. RS interval >100 ms in any precordial lead? → VT.
  3. AV dissociation (find P waves)? → VT.
  4. Morphology criteria for V1/V6 consistent with VT (e.g., R wave in aVR with initial R > QRS).  
  If any step positive → VT. If all four negative → SVT possible.

- **Key tests:**
  - **12-lead ECG:** *What it shows:* Rate, regularity, QRS width, axis, morphology, presence of capture/fusion beats, AV dissociation. *Why you order it:* The single most important test. *How to interpret:* Fast way – look at lead aVR: an initial dominant R wave (R/S ratio >1) strongly favours VT. Look for a notch in the descending limb of the QRS in leads where it appears to be a “monophasic” pattern. 
  - **Continuous ECG monitoring:** *What it shows:* Rhythm stability, potential degeneration to VF. *Why:* You need to assess response to treatment in real time.
  - **Basic bloods:** FBC, U&Es (K+, Mg2+), troponin (high sensitivity), TSH, digoxin level. *Why:* Exclude reversible causes. *Interpretation:* K+ <3.5 or >5.5 is a common trigger; troponin rise means ongoing ischaemia; low Mg2 is a co-factor.
  - **Echocardiogram:** *What it shows:* LV function, wall motion abnormalities, valve disease, structural heart disease. *Why:* Determines need for ICD. A patient with LVEF ≤35% on optimal medical therapy qualifies for primary prevention ICD.
  - **Electrophysiology study:** Not in the acute setting, but later for sustained VT with structural disease. Programmed stimulation can induce VT to map the circuit and guide ablation.

- **Pattern recognition: single finding that *confirms* VT?** AV dissociation (clearly seen P waves separate from QRS) is pathognomonic. A capture beat or fusion beat is also diagnostic. On the ECG, look for a “compensatory pause” after a PVC in the middle of a wide-complex tachycardia (capture beat → pause → resumption). That’s VT until proven otherwise.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
- **Immediate (first hour):** The goal is rhythm control while maintaining stability.
  - **If monomorphic and stable:** 
    - **Amiodarone** 300mg IV over 20–30 minutes (preferred in ischaemic heart disease, LV dysfunction). Then 900mg in 50 mL D5W over 24h (continuous infusion). *Why:* Broad-spectrum antiarrhythmic with low proarrhythmia risk. Slows conduction, prolongs repolarisation.
    - Alternative: **Procainamide** 10–15 mg/kg IV over 30 minutes (monomorphic VT, no structural heart disease). *Why:* Class 1a, effective, but can cause hypotension and QT prolongation.
    - Alternative: **Lidocaine** 1–1.5 mg/kg IV bolus (use only if acute MI, but less effective than amiodarone).
  - **Synchronised cardioversion:** If the patient becomes unstable (chest pain, BP <90, signs of shock) during your assessment, do **not** wait for drugs — synchronise at 200J (biphasic) under sedation (e.g., midazolam 0.05 mg/kg IV). 
  - **Pitfall:** *Do not* use verapamil or diltiazem (calcium channel blockers) for a wide-complex tachycardia unless you are 100% sure it’s SVT. In VT, these can cause profound hypotension and collapse because they decrease contractility and peripheral resistance. *Do not* use adenosine unless you have a defibrillator immediately available — it may accelerate degenerated VT to VF.

- **Ward (24–72h):**
  - Correct reversible causes: potassium to ≥4.5 mmol/L, magnesium to ≥2.0 mg/dL, stop offending drugs (digoxin, tricyclics). If acute MI, revascularise.
  - Start oral antiarrhythmics: **Amiodarone** 400 mg TID for the first week then reduce to 200 mg daily. Monitor for bradycardia, QT prolongation, thyroid/liver function.
  - Consider beta-blockers if LVEF is preserved (bisoprolol 2.5–5 mg daily, carvedilol 3.125–25 mg BID). *Why:* Reduce sympathetic drive, suppress triggers.
  - Arrange echocardiogram and, if LVEF ≤35% after 3 months of optimal therapy, refer for ICD implantation.

- **Long-term:**
  - **ICD implantation** for any patient with sustained VT and structural heart disease (especially ischaemic cardiomyopathy, dilated cardiomyopathy). *Why:* Only therapy that decreases arrhythmic death.
  - **Catheter ablation** for recurrent monomorphic VT despite antiarrhythmics or for idiopathic VT (RVOT, fascicular). It can cure the arrhythmia.
  - **Medications** as bridge to ICD or if ICD not appropriate. Oral amiodarone plus beta-blocker is standard for advanced heart failure.

- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
  - Do not give digoxin for acute VT — it may cause more arrhythmias.
  - Do not use lidocaine as monotherapy except in acute MI with pulseless VT.
  - Do not rely solely on ECG “morphology” to call it SVT unless the QRS is strictly <0.12s — wide is always VT until proven.
  - Do not discharge patient without an echocardiogram.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Ventricular Tachycardia (stable) | SVT with aberrancy (e.g., AVNRT, AVRT) | Atrial fibrillation with pre-excited WPW | Hyperkalaemia (wide QRS) |
|---------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Pain** | Usually none, but can have chest tightness | Usually palpitations, may have chest tightness | Palpitations ± syncope | None; likely weak, altered mental status |
| **Exam** | Variable BP but stable; may have cannon A waves | Often hypotension if severe but rate >150; signs of HF | Very fast (>250), may be unstable | Bradypnoea, muscle weakness |
| **ECG** | Wide QRS (>0.12 s), regular; search for AV dissociation, capture/fusion beats | Wide QRS but *typical* bundle branch block pattern (RS in V4-V6); no capture beats; AV dissociation absent | Irregular wide QRS with variable width; shape changes; no P waves | Very wide QRS with “sine wave” pattern; tall T waves; no P waves |
| **Labs** | Troponin may be elevated if ischaemic | Usually normal | Normal | K+ ≥6.5 mmol/L |
| **Key diff** | AV dissociation + capture/fusion beats = VT | SVT resolves with adenosine (unless atrial flutter/fibrillation with WPW) | Resembles atrial fib but QRS wide; ask about WPW diagnosis | Sine wave pattern, rapid deterioration; emergency treatment is calcium + insulin+dextrose |

- **Key distinguishing algorithm:** If uncertain about the wide-complex tachycardia, do not give verapamil. Use the Brugada algorithm on the 12-lead ECG. If you still can’t decide, treat as VT until proven otherwise.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Degeneration to ventricular fibrillation:** The most feared complication. If VT persists untreated, the myocardial oxygen demand outweighs supply, the circuit may break into chaotic ventricular fibrillation. *How it presents:* sudden loss of consciousness, pulselessness, arrest. *Key signs:* ECG shows coarse then fine VF. *What to do:* immediate defibrillation (200J biphasic), ALS algorithm.
- **Haemodynamic collapse:** Even if the rhythm is stable initially, prolonged VT reduces stroke volume and may cause cardiogenic shock. *How it presents:* progressive hypotension, cold extremities, pulmonary oedema, raised JVP. *Key signs:* lactate rising >2 mmol/L, urine output falling. *What to do:* synchronised cardioversion as soon as sedation is available.
- **Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy:** If VT is sustained for weeks (e.g., incessant idiopathic VT), the ventricle remodels and ejection fraction drops. *Pattern:* The patient has NYHA III symptoms, and ECG shows fast wide QRS. *What to do:* urgent ablation or rate control, no medication alone sufficient.
- **Myocardial infarction:** VT can cause a demand-driven ischaemia; if it occurs right after an MI, it may extend the infarct. *Key signs:* chest pain, ST elevations on ECG during tachycardia. *What to do:* treat VT first (amiodarone or cardioversion), then angioplasty.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonic for morphological clues to VT:**
  - **B**rugada steps: **A**BSENCE of RS in all precordial leads → VT.
  - **R**S interval >100 ms in any precordial lead → VT.
  - **A**V dissociation → VT.
  - **M**orphology criteria (V1/V6) → VT. (B-R-A-M)

- **Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
  1. **Capture beats = VT until proven otherwise.** A narrow beat in the middle of a wide-complex tachycardia is gold.
  2. **Lead aVR:** an initial dominant R wave with amplitude > the S wave is 90% specific for VT.
  3. **R-wave peak time in lead II** >50 ms (from onset of QRS to peak) strongly favours VT.
  4. **Monophasic or biphasic QRS in V1 with R > S** is VT (for a right bundle branch block pattern).
  5. **If QRS is >0.16 s** (very wide), it’s highly likely VT.
  6. **Positive QRS concordance** (all leads positive or all negative in precordial leads) = VT.
  7. **Never give verapamil** for a wide-complex tachycardia unless you are 100% certain it’s SVT. When in doubt, cardioversion is safer.

- **Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
  - Calling a wide QRS tachycardia “SVT with aberrancy” just because the patient is young and healthy. Aberrancy is less common than VT in sick patients.
  - Waiting for a “perfect” ECG before treating – if you see a wide QRS >0.12 s, rate >150, and the patient is stable but has CAD, start amiodarone immediately while completing your assessment.
  - Forgetting magnesium – always check and replete Mg >2.0 mg/dL before attempting long-term rhythm control.
  - Not synchronising cardioversion – an unsynchronised shock can cause VF (R-on-T).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / cardiology team immediately if:**
  - The patient has sustained VT (lasting >30 seconds without reverting).
  - There is any sign of instability (systolic BP <90 mmHg, chest pain, syncope, SPO2 <90%, respiratory distress, GCS <13).
  - The rhythm degenerates to VF (then call crash team).
  - First dose of amiodarone does not terminate VT after 20 minutes.
  - There is suspicion of acute MI.

- **Thresholds for ICU / critical care involvement:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially respiratory rate >25, BP <90, new confusion).
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L and rising.
  - Troponin rise >10× ULN.
  - Refractory VT (storm) – more than three episodes in 24 hours despite antiarrhythmics.
  - GCS <15 or seizure activity.
  - Need for continuous IV antiarrhythmic infusion and monitoring >4 hours.

**Final thought for your personal bank:** In stable VT, you have a window. Use it to get a proper 12-lead ECG, look for AV dissociation, correct electrolytes, and start amiodarone. The moment stability wavers — cardiovert. And never forget: **Wide QRS tachycardia is VT until proven otherwise.**

| Feature | Ventricular Tachycardia (stable) | SVT with aberrancy (e.g., AVNRT, AVRT) | Atrial fibrillation with pre-excited WPW | Hyperkalaemia (wide QRS) |
|---------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Pain** | Usually none, but can have chest tightness | Usually palpitations, may have chest tightness | Palpitations ± syncope | None; likely weak, altered mental status |
| **Exam** | Variable BP but stable; may have cannon A waves | Often hypotension if severe but rate >150; signs of HF | Very fast (>250), may be unstable | Bradypnoea, muscle weakness |
| **ECG** | Wide QRS (>0.12 s), regular; search for AV dissociation, capture/fusion beats | Wide QRS but *typical* bundle branch block pattern (RS in V4-V6); no capture beats; AV dissociation absent | Irregular wide QRS with variable width; shape changes; no P waves | Very wide QRS with “sine wave” pattern; tall T waves; no P waves |
| **Labs** | Troponin may be elevated if ischaemic | Usually normal | Normal | K+ ≥6.5 mmol/L |
| **Key diff** | AV dissociation + capture/fusion beats = VT | SVT resolves with adenosine (unless atrial flutter/fibrillation with WPW) | Resembles atrial fib but QRS wide; ask about WPW diagnosis | Sine wave pattern, rapid deterioration; emergency treatment is calcium + insulin+dextrose |

- **Key distinguishing algorithm:** If uncertain about the wide-complex tachycardia, do not give verapamil. Use the Brugada algorithm on the 12-lead ECG. If you still can’t decide, treat as VT until proven otherwise.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
- **Degeneration to ventricular fibrillation:** The most feared complication. If VT persists untreated, the myocardial oxygen demand outweighs supply, the circuit may break into chaotic ventricular fibrillation. *How it presents:* sudden loss of consciousness, pulselessness, arrest. *Key signs:* ECG shows coarse then fine VF. *What to do:* immediate defibrillation (200J biphasic), ALS algorithm.
- **Haemodynamic collapse:** Even if the rhythm is stable initially, prolonged VT reduces stroke volume and may cause cardiogenic shock. *How it presents:* progressive hypotension, cold extremities, pulmonary oedema, raised JVP. *Key signs:* lactate rising >2 mmol/L, urine output falling. *What to do:* synchronised cardioversion as soon as sedation is available.
- **Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy:** If VT is sustained for weeks (e.g., incessant idiopathic VT), the ventricle remodels and ejection fraction drops. *Pattern:* The patient has NYHA III symptoms, and ECG shows fast wide QRS. *What to do:* urgent ablation or rate control, no medication alone sufficient.
- **Myocardial infarction:** VT can cause a demand-driven ischaemia; if it occurs right after an MI, it may extend the infarct. *Key signs:* chest pain, ST elevations on ECG during tachycardia. *What to do:* treat VT first (amiodarone or cardioversion), then angioplasty.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
- **Mnemonic for morphological clues to VT:**
  - **B**rugada steps: **A**BSENCE of RS in all precordial leads → VT.
  - **R**S interval >100 ms in any precordial lead → VT.
  - **A**V dissociation → VT.
  - **M**orphology criteria (V1/V6) → VT. (B-R-A-M)

- **Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
  1. **Capture beats = VT until proven otherwise.** A narrow beat in the middle of a wide-complex tachycardia is gold.
  2. **Lead aVR:** an initial dominant R wave with amplitude > the S wave is 90% specific for VT.
  3. **R-wave peak time in lead II** >50 ms (from onset of QRS to peak) strongly favours VT.
  4. **Monophasic or biphasic QRS in V1 with R > S** is VT (for a right bundle branch block pattern).
  5. **If QRS is >0.16 s** (very wide), it’s highly likely VT.
  6. **Positive QRS concordance** (all leads positive or all negative in precordial leads) = VT.
  7. **Never give verapamil** for a wide-complex tachycardia unless you are 100% certain it’s SVT. When in doubt, cardioversion is safer.

- **Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
  - Calling a wide QRS tachycardia “SVT with aberrancy” just because the patient is young and healthy. Aberrancy is less common than VT in sick patients.
  - Waiting for a “perfect” ECG before treating – if you see a wide QRS >0.12 s, rate >150, and the patient is stable but has CAD, start amiodarone immediately while completing your assessment.
  - Forgetting magnesium – always check and replete Mg >2.0 mg/dL before attempting long-term rhythm control.
  - Not synchronising cardioversion – an unsynchronised shock can cause VF (R-on-T).

### 10. Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / cardiology team immediately if:**
  - The patient has sustained VT (lasting >30 seconds without reverting).
  - There is any sign of instability (systolic BP <90 mmHg, chest pain, syncope, SPO2 <90%, respiratory distress, GCS <13).
  - The rhythm degenerates to VF (then call crash team).
  - First dose of amiodarone does not terminate VT after 20 minutes.
  - There is suspicion of acute MI.

- **Thresholds for ICU / critical care involvement:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially respiratory rate >25, BP <90, new confusion).
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L and rising.
  - Troponin rise >10× ULN.
  - Refractory VT (storm) – more than three episodes in 24 hours despite antiarrhythmics.
  - GCS <15 or seizure activity.
  - Need for continuous IV antiarrhythmic infusion and monitoring >4 hours.

**Final thought for your personal bank:** In stable VT, you have a window. Use it to get a proper 12-lead ECG, look for AV dissociation, correct electrolytes, and start amiodarone. The moment stability wavers — cardiovert. And never forget: **Wide QRS tachycardia is VT until proven otherwise.**
